2: The accumulation of livestock manure (LSM) has associated some hygienic and environmental problems due to its high po- tential for pollution and high production. In this sense, subterra- nean and surface water, ground and air contamination, odors and greenhouse gases and ammonia emissions are some of the prob- lems of LSM accumulation. LSM also is a potential source of path- ogens [1]. Traditional uses of LSM, as a fertilizer and landfill, have to be changed due to land limitations and more strict regulations [1]. Currently, there is an increasing interest in the valorization of manure as a solid fuel. Therefore, the utilization of this surplus of manure for waste-to-bioenergy generation could be a sustainable choice since it is considered a zero-cost feedstock [2].
Generally, the conversion of biomass waste into energy could be carried out through biological or thermochemical processes. One of the most common biological processes is the Anaerobic Digestion

(AD). AD is usually used for odor control, manure stabilization, resulting in the generation of biogas and residual digested [3]. On the other hand, among the different thermochemical processes such as pyrolysis, combustion and thermal liquefaction, the biomass gasification is one of the key technologies to convert biomass waste into syngas as well as fuel gas, which can be used for feeding gas engines and gas turbines [4]. Biomass gasification can be defined the as the conversion of biomass into a gaseous fuel by heating in a partial oxidation atmosphere. Specifically, the gasifi- cation of the char has a special interest because it is the rate- controlling step in the gasification process [5]. Guizani et al. (2013) studied the mixtures of steam and carbon dioxide as a gasifying agent with a biomass feedstock of beech wood chips [6]. Valin et al. (2015) investigated the influence of using H2O/CO2 during the wood chips gasification process on the products yield and the remaining solid characteristics [7]. On the other hand, Scala (2015) studied the char gasification using both H2O and CO2 as gasifying agent, a kinetic model of the process being proposed based on experimental data [8]. The oxy-fuel combustion is also being studied currently. This kind of process consists of using a mixture atmosphere in which steam, oxygen and carbon dioxide

are mixed in different proportions. Su et al. (2015) focused their study on the oxy-fuel combustion with CO2 of a bituminous coal and the effect of the presence of carbon dioxide on the character- istics of the char formed [9].

Therefore, the nature of the gasifying agent and its proportion has a great influence on the gasification process. In addition, the use of CO2 would contribute to a reduction of its concentration in the atmosphere [6]. Carbon dioxide used in this type of reaction could be part of either an effluent stream coming from anaerobic di- gestions or coming from a flue gas from any combustion or gasifi- cation plants [10].

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is an appropriated tool for studying these thermochemical processes from an energetic, environmental and economic point of view [2]. In this sense, Iribarren et al. (2013) studied the life-cycle assessment of an energy conversion system for the coproduction of fuels and electricity from a gasification- based biosyngas feedstock via Fischer-Tropsch synthesis coupled with a combined-cycle process [11]. Furthermore, Susmozas et al. (2013) evaluated the hydrogen production via indirect gasification of poplar biomass, following a LCA approach [12]. Specifically, Wu et al. (2013) compared the management of manure by a gasification process to its land application, through a LCA methodology [13].

In this work, the gasification process and the gases evolved of two different manure chars (dairy and swine manure) were studied by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) coupled with mass spectrometry (MS). The influence of the amount of CO2 in the gasification process was carried out by varying the concentration of CO2 from 15 to 90 vol.%. Furthermore, the effect of AD pretreatment on the thermal behavior of dairy manure sample was evaluated. Finally, a LCA was performed to estimate and compare the gasification of the different chars considered from the viewpoint of the energy requirements, the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and the economic feasibility.

7: Two samples of biomass were used in this investigation. Swine (SW) and dairy samples were solid animal wastes obtained from the province of Québec (Canada). They were treated by bio-drying and anaerobic digestion, respectively. To evaluate the changes during the biological process, two dairy samples were studied: the storage tank output (Pre) and the digester output (Dig R) [2].

Ten grams of these manure samples were pyrolyzed in a tubular reactor in order to obtain the manure char for the gasification process. The samples were devolatilized under a continuous He flow of 200 Nml/min from room temperature to 980 °C, using a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The samples were kept around 980 °C for 1 h. He flow was maintained until the temperature dropped to ambient in order to prevent reaction with air. Once the different
chars were obtained, they were sieved to an average particle size between 50 and 100 um.
8: The gasification of manure samples were carried out in a TGA apparatus (TGA-DSC 1, METTLER TOLEDO). Previous to the gasifi- cation process, a pyrolysis process was required. Firstly, a pyrolysis step was performed in a tubular furnace as it was explained in section 2.1. Secondly, a pyrolysis stage was performed at 900 °C in the termobalance, being just a heating of the pyrolyzed sample under an inert gas to reach to the gasification temperature. Sample weight was fixed at 7 mg and the particle size was kept in the 50-100 um range. Samples were heated from ambient to 900 using a heating rate of 40 °C/min followed by 5 min at 900 under an Ar flow of 100 mL/min. Once the gasification temperature was reached, the corresponding vol.% of gasifying agent (CO2) was injected into the Ar flow (before the entrance to the furnace) during 30 min until the end of the gasification process.
The experimental error for the weight loss and temperature measurements was +0.5% and +2 °C, respectively.
The analysis of the gas produced during gasification process was conducted in a mass spectrometer (Thermostar-GSD 320/quadru- pole mass analyzer; PFEIFFER VACUUM).

10: Chars obtained from manure samples were characterized by elemental analysis, TGA, bomb calorimetry and atomic emission spectroscopy inductively coupled plasma (ICP-AES).

The ultimate analysis was used to measure the carbon (C), hydrogen (H), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and sulfur (S) content of a sample. This analysis was performed in an elemental analyzer following the standard UNE-EN 15104:2011 and was expressed as a mass percentage of each element in dry basis.

Moisture, ash, fixed carbon and volatile matter content of the samples were obtained by proximate analysis according to ASTM D 3172-73(84) standard [14]. These are the four most important chemical characteristics in any type of fuel. The equipment used to perform the proximate analysis was a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA /DSC Model 1 METTLER TOLEDO STARe System).

The heat of combustion was determined using a Parr 1356 bomb calorimeter according to UNE 164001:2005 EX at constant volume and a reference temperature of 25 °C. The energy equivalent was determined with a standard reference sample of benzoic acid. This way, a known mass amount of the sample was introduced in a gelatin capsule and combusted under an oxygen atmosphere.

11: Chars obtained from manure samples were characterized by elemental analysis, TGA, bomb calorimetry and atomic emission spectroscopy inductively coupled plasma (ICP-AES).

The ultimate analysis was used to measure the carbon (C), hydrogen (H), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and sulfur (S) content of a sample. This analysis was performed in an elemental analyzer following the standard UNE-EN 15104:2011 and was expressed as a mass percentage of each element in dry basis.

Moisture, ash, fixed carbon and volatile matter content of the samples were obtained by proximate analysis according to ASTM D 3172-73(84) standard [14]. These are the four most important chemical characteristics in any type of fuel. The equipment used to perform the proximate analysis was a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA /DSC Model 1 METTLER TOLEDO STARe System).

The heat of combustion was determined using a Parr 1356 bomb calorimeter according to UNE 164001:2005 EX at constant volume and a reference temperature of 25 °C. The energy equivalent was determined with a standard reference sample of benzoic acid. This way, a known mass amount of the sample was introduced in a gelatin capsule and combusted under an oxygen atmosphere.

15: LCA methodology presented in this study was used to estimate and compare gas emissions, energy and economic impacts of the gasification process of manure samples. In this work, the LCA of the gasification process of dairy manure samples (Pre and Dig R) was performed. Two scenarios were considered: gasification of char Pre and gasification of char Dig R (scenarios 1 and 2, respectively). The system boundaries and the two scenarios considered are shown in Fig. 1. The design basis for the gasification process was undertaken. In this sense, the whole process presented three main stages: pre- treatment of biomass (dried and crushed), reaction and recon- ditioning of products. The gasification process was carried out in a gasifier, leading to the decomposition of manure into two streams: ashes and gas. Ashes can be revalued as additives for cement or building materials. Gas stream was fed into a turbine where it was

expanded. The energy was produced by the alternator, which was connected to the network through a parallel power plant park. In LCA studies, the functional unit (FU) quantifies the function of the product system and provides a reference unit [21]. In this work, the FU of the LCA was a production basis of 10,000 tons/year of dried manure. Gas emissions and particularly greenhouse gases (GHG) released during the thermochemical process were analyzed by means of TGA-MS. The gasification process was simulated using a flowsheet simulator (Aspen Plus 8.4 licensed by Aspen Technol- ogy, Inc.), which can be used to calculate the main energy and material streams associated with the gasification of manure sam- ples. In this work, the Aspen Plus simulator was used to obtain the inputs/outputs of the energy balance. The anaerobic digester was not simulated. In this case, the data used in the economic study (production of biogas by anaerobic digestion) was extracted from real experiments carried out in Canada.

Finally, an economic evaluation of the two scenarios considered was performed. Cost of the main equipment was obtained from suppliers whereas the total fixed capital was calculated from the major equipment cost, multiplying the corresponding Lang factors according to the nature of the item. The total production cost was calculated as the sum of the depreciation (it includes the amorti- zation of the fixed capital and also the property tax) plus the direct production costs (raw materials and utilities whose consumption was calculated from the power of the process). Labor includes the manpower necessary for the correct operation of the process and the general costs of supervision and management. Other costs could be calculated by factors previously defined [22]. Net Price Value (NPV), the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) and the Payback are the techno-economic parameters used for analyzing the economic viability of the investment and the project.

R50 values and times to achieve the 50 and 99% of char con- version (X50 and X99, respectively) are summarized in Table 3.

As it can be observed in Fig. 2a, the gasification of the char started as soon as the gasifying agent reached the surface of the char. According to the parameters of Table 3, the reactivity of the different chars was ranked as follows: char Dig R > char Pre > char SW. The amount of ashes that remain after the process was of 60, 57 and 39 wt. %, respectively. According to Di Blasi et al. (2009), the char gasification process ideally takes place at a constant rate, which slowly decreases as the char is being gasified [16]. Several deviations from the ideal behavior could be observed in the DTG profiles of manure samples. Char Dig R showed a continuous in- crease up to time values of 4.5 min followed by a sudden drop. On the other hand, the char Pre DTG profile obtained the maximum rate at low times (2.5 min) with a decrease of the rate afterwards. Char SW showed the DTG maximum at a similar time than that observed with char Pre. However, the decrease of the rate showed an almost flat profile until gasification times of 11.5 min. From this time the rate started to decrease.

Char conversion is a heterogeneous process in which the chemical reactions take place over the surface of the material [16]. The reactivity of the samples depends on three main characteris- tics: the sample chemical structure, the porosity and the inorganic

Biomass sample|X99 (min)|X50 (min)|R50 (1/min) Char Pre|22.7|9.48|0.21 Char Dig R|13.0|8.23|0.31 Char SW|26.8|7.85|0.12

components [16]. The concentration of the gasifying agent also plays an important role in the process. In this regard, the concen- tration of the reactive gas was maintained constant for all experi- ments. Therefore, the sample reactivity was only attributed to the surface area and the catalytic activity of indigenous inorganic matter in the biomass [16]. In order to evaluate the influence of the char structure in the process, nitrogen adsorption/desorption iso- therms were performed. Table SS1 shows the BET surface area and the micropore volume for the char samples studied. The highest surface area (47.6 n2/g) corresponded to the most reactive sample (Dig R) which was in agreement with the trend observed previously for R50, X50 and X99 parameters. Consequently, their gasification took place at higher rates [25]. However, this fact does not seem to completely explain the gasification behavior of the samples under study, as it does not provide information about the differences found in the gasification profiles. In this regard, indigenous inor- ganic matter present in the biomass composition had an important role during the gasification process due to their catalytic nature and the different shapes of the gasification rates profiles that are commonly attributed to them [25-27]. In order to evaluate the catalytic effect of inorganic matter, typical reactivity and gasification rates versus conversion profiles were plotted in Fig. 2c and d. It can be observed that the reactivity profiles showed a sudden rise at high conversion values, which is usually found in biomass chars or alkali catalyzed carbons [5,25,28]. The increase in the reactivity was associated with the fact that the carbon material was consumed during the gasification process. Therefore, the metal to carbon ratio increased and the catalytic effect was strengthened [29,30]. As expected, the higher the ash content (Table 1) of the sample (Dig R > Pre > SW), the more prominent the increase of the samples reactivity at high conversion

values was [29,30]. Concerning the gasification rates profiles, SW showed a flat profile which is usually related to a low catalytic activity as found for other biomass chars in previous studies [5]. On the other hand, char Dig R showed an increase of the reactivity, obtaining the maximum value at a conversion around 0.75 whereas for char Pre it showed at lower values (X = 0.22).

(2009), the char gasification process ideally takes place at a constant rate, which slowly decreases as the char is being gasified [16]. Several deviations from the ideal behavior could be observed in the DTG profiles of manure samples. Char Dig R showed a continuous in- crease up to time values of 4.5 min followed by a sudden drop. On the other hand, the char Pre DTG profile obtained the maximum rate at low times (2.5 min) with a decrease of the rate afterwards. Char SW showed the DTG maximum at a similar time than that observed with char Pre. However, the decrease of the rate showed an almost flat profile until gasification times of 11.5 min. From this time the rate started to decrease.

values was [29,30]. Concerning the gasification rates profiles, SW showed a flat profile which is usually related to a low catalytic activity as found for other biomass chars in previous studies [5]. It can be observed that the main product obtained was CO, which is pro- duced by CO2 gasification of the char (C (char) + CO2 2CO). COS was detected up to gasification times close to those obtained for the full gasification of manure samples. On the other hand, the emission of SO2 started after COS was detected. This fact might point out that SO2 was produced by a further oxidation of COS. Furthermore, the maximum peak for SO2 production took place once the COS yield started decreasing. 30: The SWOT matrix concerning the use of manure as fertilizer is shown in Fig. 4.
SWOT analysis is only a tool to evaluate the strengths, weak- nesses, opportunities and threats based on a wide knowledge of the present situation and the future trends of the market [32].

In this case, the SWOT analysis was applied to decide whether the use of manure as fertilizer is the most appropriate use to this type of biomass. According to the SWOT matrix, there are more weaknesses than strengths in the use of manure as fertilizer. As it can be seen, the item "high manure production" was placed in both

the strengths and the weaknesses. The high production is a strength since the viewpoint of a continuous raw material but also is a weakness because there is more production than the fertilizer market can assume. Moreover, land limitations are one of the threats.

Therefore, by considering manure as a raw material of a gasifi- cation process, the strength and the opportunities were the same as in the case of fertilizers and the majority of the weaknesses and the threats (pollution, contamination and the strict regulation) could be solved.

42: Concerning the time to achieve the 99% of char conversion, the reactivity of the samples was ranked as follows: char Dig R > char Pre > char SW. The reactivity of the samples mainly depended on the chemical structure, porosity and inorganic components. Ac- cording to the results obtained, the higher surface area (47.6 m²/g) corresponded to the most reactive sample (char Dig R). Further- more, the highest ash content of the samples also corresponded to char Dig R (59.28%), which corroborated the higher reactivity of this sample at high conversion values. The gas product distribution detected with the TGA-MS technique was similar for all samples. The main gaseous products released during CO2 gasification pro- cess were CO, H2, SOx, C2H2 and C2H5. Concerning the LCA study, the gasification of char Pre was the most viable scenario from the economic and environmental point of view and the gasification of the char Dig R was the best energetic option. To sum up, the gasi- fication of manure is a better option against its traditional use as fertilizer since the weaknesses of the latter use is solved.
